title.value: An Extensive Overview of Diagnostic Captioning: Methods, Datasets, and Evaluation Measures
title.evidence: An Extensive Overview of Diagnostic Captioning: Methods, Datasets, and Evaluation Measures

authors.evidence: John Pavlopoulos1,* Vasiliki Kougia1,2, Ion Androutsopoulos2, and Dimitris Papamichail3

year.evidence: This article is an extensive overview of DC.
year.notes: Assumed publication year based on context.

abstract.value: Diagnostic Captioning (DC) concerns the automatic generation of a diagnostic text from a set of medical images of a patient collected during an examination. DC can assist inexperienced physicians, reducing clinical errors. It can also help experienced physicians produce diagnostic reports faster. Following the advances of deep learning, especially in generic image captioning, DC has recently attracted more attention, leading to several systems and datasets. This article is an extensive overview of DC. It presents relevant datasets, evaluation measures, and up to date systems. It also highlights shortcomings that hinder DC’s progress and proposes future directions.
abstract.evidence: Diagnostic Captioning (DC) concerns the automatic generation of a diagnostic text from a set of medical images of a patient collected during an examination. DC can assist inexperienced physicians, reducing clinical errors. It can also help experienced physicians produce diagnostic reports faster. Following the advances of deep learning, especially in generic image captioning, DC has recently attracted more attention, leading to several systems and datasets. This article is an extensive overview of DC. It presents relevant datasets, evaluation measures, and up to date systems. It also highlights shortcomings that hinder DC’s progress and proposes future directions.

doi.notes: No DOI provided in the paper.

venue.evidence: This article is an extensive overview of DC.

problem_statement.value: The increasing workload of radiologists, due to the rising number of medical imaging examinations, increases the likelihood of clinical errors. There is a need for tools that assist radiologists in generating high-quality diagnostic reports efficiently.
problem_statement.evidence: Radiologists need to consider the examination’s medical images, patient history, previous examinations, consult recent bibliography, and prepare a medical report. An increased workload in this demanding task increases the likelihood of medical errors.

method_type.evidence: DC methods usually employ the encoder-decoder architecture, heavily ignoring retrieval-based approaches.
method_type.notes: The paper emphasizes the dual approach of deep learning and retrieval-based methods.

architecture.value: Encoder-Decoder Architecture with potential use of Retrieval-Based Methods
architecture.evidence: The most common system architecture in DC is the encoder-decoder approach.
architecture.notes: The encoder-decoder approach is central to many DC systems.

training_data.evidence: We focus on the latter two quality datasets, MIMIC-CXR and IU X-RAY.
training_data.notes: These datasets are critical for training DC systems.

evaluation_metrics.evidence: Texts generated by DC systems have so far been assessed mostly via automatic evaluation measures originating from machine translation and text summarization.
evaluation_metrics.notes: These metrics are commonly used but may not fully capture clinical correctness.

experiment_design.value: Comparison of various DC methods using publicly available datasets and evaluation metrics.
experiment_design.evidence: We provide a segmentation of current DC systems by the kinds of methods they use, also reporting evaluation scores for each system with all available measures.
experiment_design.notes: The experiments focus on comparing different methodologies.

value: Retrieval-based methods outperform complex encoder-decoder systems in clinical recall.
The encoder-decoder approach may be less effective than retrieval-based approaches.
results.evidence: Even the simple nearest neighbor approach... has been reported to outperform all other approaches in clinical recall.
results.notes: Highlights the effectiveness of simpler methods in certain contexts.

value: Hybrid methods combining retrieval and generation are promising.
comparison_with_baselines.evidence: The very high manual evaluation score of the retrieval-based method of Li et al. (2019) is an indication that the encoder-decoder approach may be worse than retrieval-based approaches to DC.
comparison_with_baselines.notes: Comparison emphasizes the advantages of retrieval-based methods.

value: Current evaluation measures often fail to capture clinical correctness.
Shortcomings in datasets limit the generalizability of findings.
challenges.evidence: Evaluation measures employed by previous DC research mainly assess lexical overlap... without directly assessing clinical correctness.
challenges.notes: Challenges highlight the need for improved evaluation metrics.

value: Develop hybrid methods that combine encoder-decoder and retrieval-based approaches.
Enhance evaluation measures to better assess clinical correctness.
Create larger, more realistic public datasets for training.
future_directions.evidence: Our main findings also guide our proposals for future work on DC, listed below.

provenance.section_header: Conclusions and Directions for Future Research

future_directions.notes: Future directions focus on addressing current limitations.

code_availability.notes: No information on code availability was provided.

dataset_access.evidence: Datasets used in previous DC work are not all publicly available.
dataset_access.notes: Access to datasets is essential for reproducibility.

experiment_details.value: The paper provides instructions on how to obtain and use the IU X-RAY dataset split.
experiment_details.evidence: We also release (in supplementary material that will accompany the camera-ready) instructions on how to obtain and use this split.
